executive_verdict: Use a focused product-demo frontend, not a broad dashboard and not a marketing landing page. The strongest buildathon interface is a guided 3-minute operational demo: seed three quality incidents, show their convergence into one canonical skill gap, explain the shift readiness formula, then create a mentor action. This is the best way to make KANIT feel like a real system instead of an AI-generated report writer.
relevance_to_kanit: The current FastAPI backend already supports the exact story the frontend must tell: /demo/seed-readiness creates the golden path, /skills/ontology and /skills/normalize prove deterministic skill normalization, /shifts/readiness returns an auditable score_breakdown, /incidents/{incident_id}/evidence-graph exposes evidence relationships, /learning-tasks/{task_id} and /mentor-reviews close the human approval loop, and /exports/readiness/{readiness_id} demonstrates integration positioning for Poka, Augmentir, LMS, QMS, BI, or generic clients.
benchmark_repos_or_products: React Flow / xyflow for node-link operational evidence graphs.
Motion for meaningful panel transitions, path reveals, and score changes.
shadcn/ui, Radix UI, Tailwind CSS, and Lucide icons for a polished but maintainable operational SaaS surface.
Linear, Vercel, Stripe Dashboard, Retool, and modern observability dashboards as UX references for dense but calm product interfaces.
Awwwards-style references should be used for craft, motion pacing, typography, and spatial rhythm, not for heavy 3D spectacle.
github_star_evidence: For this architecture item, GitHub star counts are secondary because the core decision is fit-to-demo and API compatibility. The broader research track should still inspect high-star repos such as vercel/next.js, tailwindlabs/tailwindcss, shadcn-ui/ui, motiondivision/motion, xyflow/xyflow, lucide-icons/lucide, pmndrs/react-three-fiber, mrdoob/three.js, d3/d3, recharts/recharts, airbnb/lottie-web, and greensock/GSAP. Stars should be treated as ecosystem confidence, not as proof that a tool is right for KANIT.
premium_design_patterns: App-first opening: show the actual readiness system immediately, not a hero page with vague claims.
One-sentence value framing above the demo: 'KANIT turns repeated quality incidents into tomorrow's shift readiness signal.'
Evidence convergence as the signature visual: three incident cards animate into one canonical skill node, with thin industrial linework and readable labels.
Formula transparency: the score panel must show 100 - penalties + bonuses = final score, with each penalty tied to evidence.
Human-in-the-loop credibility: mentor review is visible as a required step, not hidden in a webhook log.
Calm operational palette: light neutral surfaces, graphite text, one blue accent, restrained amber/red/green status colors, and no decorative orbs.
Microcopy should sound like a factory readiness tool, not a generic AI assistant.

recommended_tools.framework: Next.js with React and TypeScript if starting a new frontend; Vite + React is acceptable if speed and simple local demo are more important than routing/server features.
recommended_tools.styling: Tailwind CSS with CSS custom properties for semantic tokens.
recommended_tools.ui_primitives: shadcn/ui plus Radix primitives for dialogs, tabs, popovers, tooltips, and accessible form controls.
recommended_tools.icons: Lucide React for compact operational actions and status affordances.
recommended_tools.motion: Motion for React for page transitions, evidence path reveal, step transitions, score count-up, and reduced-motion support.
recommended_tools.graph: React Flow for the Evidence Graph if editable/inspectable nodes are needed; custom SVG is better for the 3-minute golden demo if the graph is static and must look highly curated.
recommended_tools.charts: Use lightweight custom SVG or Recharts only for small score breakdown bars. Avoid a full analytics charting stack for the buildathon demo.
recommended_tools.assets: Use custom inline SVG linework for station, incident, skill, and mentor artifacts. Avoid stock illustrations.
recommended_tools.testing: Playwright screenshots for desktop and mobile demo states; axe or equivalent accessibility checks if dependencies allow.

recommended_skills: research-deep for tool and benchmark validation before final stack selection.
superpowers:brainstorming before locking the experience and interaction model.
ui-ux-pro-max for accessibility, responsive layout, motion restraint, and interaction quality.
ckm-design and ckm-ui-styling for visual system, tokens, typography, and production polish.
design-review or qa after implementation to catch overlap, weak hierarchy, motion noise, and mobile issues.

use: Animate the user's understanding, not decoration: incident cards flow into the canonical skill node, then the score formula unfolds.
Use 150-300 ms transitions for panels and controls; use a slightly longer 600-900 ms path draw only for the main evidence convergence moment.
Animate transform and opacity instead of layout-changing properties.
Use subtle background grid or process-line SVG at very low contrast to imply operational systems without visual noise.
Respect prefers-reduced-motion by showing the final graph state without path drawing.
avoid: No decorative gradient orbs, bokeh blobs, AI-looking glassmorphism overload, random 3D scenes, or animated backgrounds that compete with the data.
No generic robot, brain, circuit-board, or chatbot illustration.
No animation that delays the 3-minute demo or hides the score explanation.

screen_model.job: Make the value obvious in five seconds and start the golden path.
screen_model.content: One sentence, one primary button, backend status, and a compact promise: incidents -> skill gap -> shift risk -> mentor action.

screen_model.job: Prove that KANIT is not merely summarizing text.
screen_model.content: Three incident nodes with alias labels route into effectiveness_verification. Show convergence_proof.aliases_seen and convergence_proof.canonical_skill_id.

screen_model.job: Answer the Ford engineer's question: how did you calculate the score?
screen_model.primary_api: readiness object returned by /demo/seed-readiness or POST /shifts/readiness.
screen_model.content: Large score, risk level, rule_version, formula, penalties, bonuses, and evidence trace. The score cannot look arbitrary.

screen_model.job: Show the human approval loop and turn risk into an operational next step.
screen_model.content: A micro-practice task, expected evidence, mentor decision buttons, and the statement that readiness is not approved until the mentor closes the loop.

secondary_surfaces: A compact API drawer for technical reviewers showing endpoint names and response fragments.
An export drawer for Poka/Augmentir/LMS/QMS positioning, kept secondary so it does not steal demo time.
A mobile layout that turns the graph into a vertical evidence chain rather than trying to shrink a wide canvas.
ux_information_architecture.copy_strategy: Lead with user benefit, then reveal technical proof. The opening line should be about reducing tomorrow's shift risk, not about ontologies, agents, or graphs.

accessibility_performance_rules: All interactive targets should be at least 44x44 px and keyboard reachable.
Use visible focus states and semantic buttons rather than div-based controls.
Do not rely on color alone for risk. Pair color with text, icon, and explanation.
Maintain at least WCAG AA contrast for body text and controls.
Reserve stable dimensions for score cards, graph nodes, and formula panels to avoid layout shift during demo playback.
Provide skeleton or staged loading states while the demo seed request is running.
Support prefers-reduced-motion and avoid continuous background animation.
Keep the first load small: avoid Three.js, Lottie, Rive, or GSAP unless a specific interaction justifies the cost.
Design mobile first, but optimize the live presentation viewport around 1366x768 and 1440x900 because that is likely where the jury will watch.

implementation_implications.api_client: Create a typed frontend API client for /health, /demo/seed-readiness, /skills/ontology, /skills/normalize, /shifts/readiness, /learning-tasks/{task_id}, /mentor-reviews, and /exports/readiness/{readiness_id}. Include X-KANIT-API-Key support through environment configuration.
implementation_implications.state_model: Use a simple demo state machine: idle -> seeding -> convergence -> readiness -> mentor_review -> exported. This is more reliable than free navigation for a 3-minute buildathon demo.
implementation_implications.data_contract: Treat readiness.score_breakdown, readiness.risk_drivers, readiness.evidence_trace, and convergence_proof as first-class UI objects. Do not flatten them into vague text.
implementation_implications.error_handling: If /health fails, show backend offline with a clear setup hint. Do not silently switch to frontend mock data because the user explicitly rejected mock fallback for production credibility.
implementation_implications.security: Do not expose real API keys in client code. For local demo, use a public demo key only if the backend is configured that way; otherwise proxy through a local server route.
implementation_implications.future_extensibility: The same frontend shell can later add uploaded 8D analysis, team learning map, skill miner, and integration export pages without changing the core story.

what_to_avoid: Avoid a generic AI SaaS landing page. The first screen must be the product experience.
Avoid a crowded dashboard with ten KPIs. The jury should remember one thing: repeated incidents became a shift readiness risk.
Avoid leading with COPQ proxy because it can look like a simple calculator without real cost integration.
Avoid showing n8n, MCP, RAG, or model plumbing as the main story. They can be technical appendix items.
Avoid claiming Ford portal integration, autonomous compliance, or certified quality decisions.
Avoid free-form LLM labels in the UI. Always show canonical skill IDs and explain normalization.
Avoid huge 3D/scroll-driven spectacle. It may win attention but weaken trust for an operational quality product.

demo_story_implications.opening_line: KANIT finds the skill gap hidden inside repeated quality incidents before tomorrow's shift starts.
three_minute_script: 0:00-0:25: Show the live app, say the problem in one sentence, click Start Demo.
0:25-1:05: Three 8D/CAPA incidents appear and converge into effectiveness_verification despite different labels.
1:05-1:50: Shift Readiness shows risk, formula, penalties, and evidence trace. Emphasize that the score is rule-based, not AI magic.
1:50-2:30: Open the mentor action and show the micro-practice tied to the real incident pattern.
2:30-3:00: Close with integration story: KANIT does not replace Poka/Augmentir/QMS; it feeds them evidence-based skill signals.
demo_story_implications.defense_line: ChatGPT can summarize an 8D case. KANIT normalizes repeated incident evidence into a canonical skill gap, calculates an auditable readiness score, and requires human approval before calling the shift ready.
frontend_success_criteria: A non-technical HR viewer understands the value in five seconds.
A Ford quality engineer can inspect the score formula and evidence trace.
A technical reviewer sees real API-backed state transitions.
The visual signature is memorable: three incidents converging into one skill risk.
